text: The first involves hot-to-cold-zone transfer of nickel in Inconel 600 pumped loops circulating liquid sodium.

Three hypothetical examples are considered, namely: (1) chromium corrosion at all points, transfer to salt only; (2) hot-to-cold-zone chromium transfer; and finally (3) cold-to-hot-zone chromium transfer.

While complete data to substantiate the results computed for the above cases are not available, the success of early $^{51}\mathrm{Cr}$ tracer experiments (example 1) suggests that the solid-state diffusion mechanism does apply to certain molten-salt systems when the salt constituents (and impurities) are subjected to stringent control.

In a previous report<sup>1</sup> (hereafter referred to as Report I) attention was given to interpretations of corrosion behavior in systems composed of liquid sodium contained in the nickel-base alloy Inconel 600.
At the time of this writing, sodium systems, which are of interest to the Liquid Metal Fast Breeder Reactor, $^{2,3}$ are under intensive study.

We should note a treatment similar to that to be covered here was initially roughed out in 1957 under the auspices of the Aircraft Nuclear Propulsion (ANP) Project. $^{4}$ The liquids of interest in this early effort were molten fluoride salts. $^{5}$
These relationships are developed elsewhere.[6,7] It is sufficient here to point out just a few important features of the equations involving $x(w, t)$ .
First, $x(0, t)$ is assumed to be constant with time.[8] Only linear diffusion along a single coordinate,[9] $w$ , shall be considered.
The direction of $w$ is normal to the liquid exposed surface, $A_z$ , where $w = 0$ .

Stated in the language of partial differential equations, the heat equation must be homogeneous; the same is true for all but one of the boundary conditions $^{10,11}$ unless an additional equation is involved.

The problem at hand requires use of $\mathbf{x}(\mathbf{w},\mathbf{t})$ as prescribed for Fick's first law, the latter being evaluated at the surface to obtain an expression for the flux traversing $\mathbf{w} = 0$ :
j _ {M} = - D \rho_ {a} \frac {\partial x (0 , t)}{\partial w} = - \rho_ {a} \triangle x (D / \pi t) ^ {1 / 2}. \tag {5}
(5) with $x(0,t) = x_{T}$ takes the form
j _ {M} = \rho_ {a} x _ {a} \left(1 - x _ {T} / x _ {a}\right) (D / \pi t) ^ {1 / 2}. \tag {6}
(5) has been cast into the form of Eq.

(6) may be integrated with respect to time without concern about the $\mathbf{x}_{\mathbf{T}} / \mathbf{x}_{\mathbf{a}}$ relationship, since $\mathbf{x}(0, t)$ and, therefore, $\mathbf{x}_{\mathbf{T}} / \mathbf{x}_{\mathbf{a}}$ do not vary with time.
\Delta M (t) / A _ {z} = \int_ {0} ^ {t} j _ {M} d t ^ {\prime} = - 2 \rho_ {a} x _ {a} \left(1 - x _ {T} / x _ {a}\right) \left(D t / \pi\right) ^ {1 / 2}. \tag {7}
(6) and (7) follow the adopted convention automatically.

The next mathematical operation involves integration along $z$ , but this requires some knowledge of the manner in which $z$ varies with $T$ and, of greater importance, the manner in which $T$ varies with $x(0,t)$ .
A good example is the reaction that gives rise to chromium migration in Inconel 600 loops circulating $\mathrm{UF}_4$ -bearing molten salts. $^{12,13}$ The reaction of interest is

\operatorname {C r} (\underline {{s}}) + 2 \operatorname {U F} _ {4} (\underline {{d}}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \operatorname {U F} _ {3} (\underline {{d}}) + \operatorname {C r F} _ {2} (\underline {{d}}). \tag {8}
\mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {T}} = \frac {[ \mathrm {C r F} _ {2} ] [ \mathrm {U F} _ {3} ] ^ {2}}{[ \mathrm {C r} ] [ \mathrm {U F} _ {4} ] ^ {2}}, \tag {9}
[ \mathrm {C r} ] = \left(\frac {[ \mathrm {U F} _ {3} ]}{[ \mathrm {U F} _ {4} ]}\right) ^ {2} \frac {[ \mathrm {C r F} _ {2} ]}{\left(\mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {T}}\right)}. \tag {9a}

where we assume the activity coefficient $\gamma$ to be unity and the mole fraction $\overline{x}$ of nickel to be unity, since the solubility experiments were conducted in a pure nickel pot. $^{16}$ In this case, Y (the saturation value in the pot experiments) is equivalent to the experimental $K_{T}$ .

The interested reader is referred to discussions given by Bird et al. $^{17}$ Of particular importance is the analogous way in which h's for mass and heat transfer are computed. $^{18}$ Many think of h as a representative of a diffusion parameter because a binary diffusion coefficient (for example, Ni(d) in sodium) appears in the correlations concerning h for mass transfer.
The relationships sought are not new.[19] We repeat them here to gain generality and completeness.

Quasi-steady state invokes two conditions: (1) that the bulk concentration of the liquid and, hence, the concentration of surface elements, $x(0,t)$ , remain fixed with time, and (2) that the effects of the liquid film resistance on mass transfer remain fixed with time.
\frac {\partial^ {2} \mathbf {x}}{\partial \mathbf {w} ^ {2}} = \frac {1}{D} \frac {\partial \mathbf {x}}{\partial t}, \tag {26}
Boundary conditions are $x(w,0) = x_a$ , $x(\infty, t) = x_a$ , $x(0,\infty) = x^*$ ; finally, $x(0,t)$ must satisfy Eq.

We have compared two values of $\mathbf{H}$ corresponding to typical maximum and minimum temperatures for experiments with Inconel 600 loops containing sodium, namely, $1089^{\circ}\mathbf{K}$ ( $1500^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ ) and $922^{\circ}\mathbf{K}$ ( $1200^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ ).
Predicted Transient Behavior in a Sodium-Inconel 600 Loop Operating at Typical Forced-Convection Conditions.
resistance become diminishingly small after about 24 hr.

Since operating times for pump loops currently range from 1000 hr to one year, we may neglect such transient effects without significantly affecting the calculation of the overall transfer of M from one zone to another.
Figure 3 shows a schematic diagram of the reference forced-convection loops used in early liquid-metal corrosion tests.

The maximum and minimum loop temperatures were $816^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ( $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ) and $649^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ( $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ), respectively, with a flow rate of 2.5 gpm and a typical operating time of 1000 hr.
The temperature profile and dimensional information for the experimental (reference) loop are given in Table 1.

Before operation, the loop was flushed with sodium at about $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for several hours to remove surface oxides.

Table 1 illustrates that the temperature profile and geometrical characteristics of the reference loop are quite complicated, especially if one wishes to apply a mathematical treatment of the diffusion process using numerical examples.

The most logical prototype that is easily envisioned is the "tent-shaped" profile over a constant-diameter loop used by Keynes.[26] This "tent-shaped" profile appears to display the utility desired, although it is somewhat awkward when applied to other mechanisms, where perhaps a saw-tooth profile might be most appropriate.

The idea that a tent-shaped profile composed of two straight-line segments cannot be experimentally obtained has been dispelled by DeVan and Sessions.[27] They used profiles that were tent-shaped but showed some asymmetry.
In Table 1 we see that the total area exposed to liquid is 784 in.² or 5050 cm².
If we assume a constant diameter of 0.70 in. all around the loop, the total length will be 906 cm, which is not too far removed from the actual value of 886 cm for the reference loop.
The average Reynolds number, $\langle N_{\mathrm{Re}}\rangle$ , weighted according to the fractional areas involved, is $5.06 \times 10^4$ .

One could stop here, insofar as the mathematics is concerned, and integrate graphically as was done in the original work.[33] The procedure adopted at that time was to assume a balance point, prepare a plot of the integrand as indicated in Fig.
5, and then ascertain the area under the hot zone portion of the curve.
6 speeded the work; but this approach required many trials, and it was tedious and boring to say the least.
This same approach was used by Epstein. $^{34}$ This difference is of considerable importance because after ten years it is still quite difficult to achieve a sinusoidal profile in the laboratory.

About the most mathematically tractable profile seen to date by the authors was that obtained recently by DeVan and Sessions $^{35}$ and by Koger and Litman. $^{36,37}$ Yet even this profile was slightly skewed.

Finally a value for $\triangle M(t)$ may be found by use of integral terms for the cold zone (limits: 1, p) and for the hot zone (limits: p, 2).

Values for both zones were computed and averaged since all the differences involved introduced some uncertainties in "hand calculations." Everything required to calculate $\triangle M(t)$ appears in Table 3 except values of C and C'.
The test period of interest is 1000 hr, $2t^{1/2} = 3.8 \times 10^{+3} \sec^{1/2}$ ; therefore:

A very high value of transport could be obtained by assuming that no balance point would exist because all the liquid could be forced through a $100\%$ -efficient nickel trap placed at the coldest point of the loop.
Accordingly, a computer program $^{41}$ was developed to handle all cases where $\mathbf{E}_{\mathrm{D}} \geq 2 \mathbf{E}_{\mathrm{soln}}$ .
9, in which computer program results for the actual loop and the prototype are presented together.

The answer to the question, as was the case for Report I, is, "No, it does not!" The amount we must account for ranges between 10 to $14\mathrm{g}$ Ni.

The computations produce values considerably less than $1\mathrm{g}$ Ni if one assumes that a balance point exists and about $4\mathrm{g}$ if one assumes that the entire loop acts as a hot zone under the dominance of the mechanism proposed.
4 g and base our conclusions on a predicted value ranging about 0.242 g.
A second possibility for increasing the predicted values would be to invoke solid-state diffusion equations where the point at which $w = 0$ moves around the loop.

However, the solution $x(w, t)$ , even for the case where the $w = 0$ boundary moves at a constant rate (a simple case),<sup>42</sup> is too complex to be effectively applied to a mathematical treatment of the mass transfer process.
A schematic diagram of the reference thermal convection loop used in molten-salt experiments, particularly the $^{51}\mathrm{Cr}$ tracer experiments,
The flow velocities in the thermal convection loop are 2 to $10\mathrm{~fpm}$ , depending on the temperature difference and dimensions of the vertical sections.
The total tubing length of the loop under discussion is $254.0\mathrm{~cm}$ and the diameter is $1.383\mathrm{~cm}$ .

The maximum temperature of the two actual (reference) thermal convection loops in which our calculations are based was $860^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ( $1580^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ) and the minimum $685^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ( $1265^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ).
Both loops were constructed of Hastelloy N, whose nominal composition is given in Table 4.
Figure 11 shows the average temperature profile for the two loops and the profile assumed for the prototype.
The prototype profile was constructed by drawing straight lines from the minimum temperature at $f = 0$ and 1.0 to the maximum temperature at $f = 0.5$ .
These values are based on determinations $^{43,44}$ of the self-diffusion of chromium in Hastelloy N using the tracer $^{51}\mathrm{Cr}$ in thermal convection loops like those previously mentioned.

As already noted, the conditions under which the calculations were made for example I represent actual conditions encountered in loop 1249, namely, an excess of $\mathrm{FeF_2}$ was added to the salt so that the entire loop acted as a hot zone.
